title: The Windows Laptop Problem

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So, two really crazy Mac laptops which came out in the past month. The MacBook Neo, which is one of the best deals in tech in a long time, 600 bucks and more efficient and more capable and better built than almost every other laptop in that price range. And the new MacBook Pros at the opposite end of the spectrum. I've been benchmarking a $6,000 16-inch M5 Max MacBook Pro, and the results are just getting ridiculous. It has multi-core CPU performance at the top of the charts for every Mac ever, and the GPU is now more powerful than the M2 Ultra in my $10,000 Mac Pro. And the new SSD's read-write speeds are upgraded, approaching 20,000 megabytes per second. It's just absurd. It's awesome. It's a creative professional's dream.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Both of these computers are putting Windows laptops on notice. And by extension, Microsoft, and Dell, and HP, and Asus, and Lenovo, and Razer, etc., all on notice.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: There was a quote recently from an Asus executive that described the new MacBook Neo as a shock to the industry. And yes, but it also got me thinking, what is actually happening with the rest of the industry? What is happening on the Windows side of the fence? So, I just started digging in. I started getting my hands on a bunch of these Windows laptops and researching and benchmarking, and it's become pretty clear, the more I think about it, that Windows versus Mac laptops is basically the same as iPhone versus Android. Because just like Android, the advantage of the Windows side is the variety and the choice. But that's also now both an advantage and a disadvantage that's getting kind of scary now.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Take this. This is the new Dell XPS 14. I used to really like XPS computers back in the day. Some of my oldest computers were XPSes. Then Dell did this thing where they killed off the XPS, the Latitude and the Inspiron names and rebranded them into Dell, Dell Pro, and Dell Pro Max for some reason. Then they came back to their senses and brought back XPS and I bought this one, and this thing is really nice. 14-inch 2.8K tandem OLED display, thin bezels all the way around with a webcam and no notch, 120 hertz variable refresh rate, and then it's this thin all-metal jacket with soft-touch finish around the palm rests, which hide a massive force touch trackpad. There's three Thunderbolt ports, there's a headphone jack, and then inside is an Intel Panther Lake processor, this one a Core Ultra 300 series with their new integrated GPUs, plus 32 gigs of DDR5 RAM and a terabyte SSD. I liked a lot of the previous XPSes, like I said, but this one is in a lot of ways better than those.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Uh, but it also does have a couple quirks. Like the ports are all Thunderbolt 4 instead of Thunderbolt 5, and there's no SD card slot or any other full-size ports, which on a premium laptop like this is a choice. There's also no lip to open the lid for some reason. So it's always this little awkward pry to like get the laptop open every single time. And then the keyboard is probably its weakest...

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: At this point, it's a bit shallow. The layout is slightly wonky with these top menu buttons, which get slightly more misaligned the further from the outside you get. But otherwise, I mean, yeah, this is really nice. So this, this is a $2,200 laptop as spec'd.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So obviously a pretty premium option. But this is, you know, just one of many premium options you could pick. Now why do I bring up this laptop? Well, in Windows land, this laptop being really good actually relies on a bunch of different companies all executing really well at the exact same time, right?

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So it relies on Dell putting a lot of effort into actually bringing back the XPS line and not cheaping out and doing the metal build quality and a nice display and the whole premium design. It also relies on Intel and this exact generation of Panther Lake chips being good and actually having good integrated graphics because they don't actually offer this laptop with a discrete GPU.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And it relies on Microsoft to actually do a good job with Windows so that the software experience is worthy of your premium price tag and not riddled with ads or unnecessary AI features. And if it all happens, then together this laptop feels like it's worth the premium price.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: But you can probably see where I'm going with this. That doesn't always happen. And lately, Windows has felt like a weak link and every single Windows laptop will have to suffer for it. Now, it's not that the whole OS is like fundamentally bad. It's not super buggy or anything crazy. It's still functional, but it's just lacking the super premium feel that wins everybody over.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Like this XPS, the first time I booted this up to set it up, it took me like 45 minutes because of all the forced updates and then all these mandatory sign-ins and it was asking me to use Microsoft 365 and then OneDrive and then all these other services, etc., etc. And then even once I got in, I got ads.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Like, I got a pop-up ad for McAfee security on this $2,000 brand new laptop, which, to be fair, is probably a Dell problem. But then that's not even mentioning all the other AI stuff that Microsoft is pushing, the Copilot stuff, the infamous Recall feature that everyone hates. There's generative AI in Microsoft Paint now, for crying out loud.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Microsoft actually requires a dedicated Copilot button on the keyboard of every new Windows 11 laptop now to be classified as an AI PC, whatever that means. Uh, so just between that and the ads, it's just not really feeling like a totally premium experience.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And at that high end, when you're spending a lot, users will start to get deservedly really picky. You're spending a lot of money, you deserve to. And that's where Apple has pretty much exclusively been playing for like the last decade. The cheapest new MacBook was always $1,000 and then it went way up from there.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And they are so vertically integrated with their Apple silicon and so the hardware is designed for the software and the software is designed for the hardware and it's always felt so efficient and clean and performant and people just love their expensive MacBooks.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Now there's obviously still reasons why people buy high-end Windows laptops. It's still a lot of the choice and the variety, and there are certain things you still can't get in Mac land, like if you want a high-end gaming laptop, you know, that's one of those common things. You can go get a Razer Blade with a 5070 and a 240 Hz display. That's an option. So there are still those things, but every one of those relies on all of the parts from all those different companies again being good at the same time, and that is a tough challenge.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So now flip it to the low end. And this is where it gets really interesting because it turns out, as we're learning, a lot of these efficiencies that Apple's been using to dominate the high end are also very effective in the low end. Because to be clear, people are absolutely willing to accept some more shortcomings in lower-priced laptops. Like that's the trade-off that's a part of the game. You know, if you shop around, hey, maybe you'll be able to find a laptop with just the right set of trade-offs that you don't mind while still being decent at the stuff you care about. Like this is the Acer Aspire 16. Super popular laptop in this segment. It's actually discounted recently, so I got it for 550 from Amazon.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And there's a few more stickers on it, yes, and there's a little more plastic and the keyboard deck flex is just a little bit lacking in build quality, yes. And the Snapdragon chip that powers it, you know, it's not going to have the highest-end performance, but it's efficient, so it has good battery. And then there's a nice big 16-inch screen and a bunch of full-size ports here. So if I'm just going to be writing or web browsing or watching videos on this thing, feels like a pretty good value. So look, laptops like this have been just fine. They've been thriving at these price points, but here's a problem. Here's the brand new Gatorade-colored MacBook Neo-shaped problem.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: The starting price for a MacBook has been $1,000 for a new machine for the past couple years. Now it just dropped to 600 and actually 500 if you have a student discount. And if you've seen the reviews, you already know it turns out that they did a great job with the Neo to making it feel just like the more premium Macs. Same clean software, same metal build, same keyboard, and more importantly, the same tight vertical integration where the hardware and the software are designed for each other and work super efficiently with each other.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So it's like unless you have a Windows-specific need, like a certain app you need to use software-wise or a certain game you want to play, most people crave the premium experience that's been associated with the Macs that are more expensive for all these years. And so the Neo coming along for 600 bucks represented that premium, well-integrated, efficient experience that is going to be incredibly hard to compete with. In Windows land, you're not only relying on all these different companies making the parts to all make them well at the same time, you're also paying them all who need their own profits to survive. And so that makes it really hard to make good, cheap laptops and actually profit.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: From that. So then the cherry on top is not only does Apple basically print A18 Pros for cheap, but also Apple's not even necessarily concerned with making a huge hardware profit on selling Neos, because this is more of a Trojan horse to create a bunch of new first-time Mac buyers who then become Apple Services subscribers.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So Apple can make this computer even cheaper than they ordinarily would to effectively acquire new Mac users who will then go subscribe to TV Plus or AppleCare or iCloud or whatever other services. And that's worth more than the hardware margin would be.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And it already seems to be working. There was just a Tim Cook tweet this week about how Mac just had its best launch ever for first-time Mac customers.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So yeah, guilty of talking about Apple a lot in this video, but it turns out the shock to the industry is very real, and it's going to be so fascinating to see what sort of responses we actually get from Windows laptop makers in the next couple months and years and what sort of strings they try to pull because if they aren't able to do anything about it, then Neos just going to keep eating their lunch and they could have a bit of a market share crisis on their hands.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Also, one more quick thing, uh we just came out with these stickers that we're adding to our merch store. I love the way these turned out. The team cooked on these. This one is a color picker sticker, which is so fun. So it's kind of an if you know you know design, but you can put them on anything colorful for fun, at least that's what I've been doing.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: You get a bunch of stickers in a pack, so go get some for yourself and send me pictures of what you stick them on. Tag me on Twitter and Threads and stuff.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: All right. Thanks for watching. Catch you guys in the next one. Peace.

highlights: The new $6,000 MacBook Pro with the M5 Max chip delivers record-breaking performance, featuring a GPU that is now more powerful than the M2 Ultra found in the $10,000 Mac Pro.
The $600 MacBook Neo represents a "shock to the industry" by bringing Apple’s premium build quality and vertical integration to a budget price point traditionally dominated by Windows laptops.

High-end Windows laptops struggle to maintain a premium feel because they rely on multiple companies—like Dell, Intel, and Microsoft—to execute perfectly at once, often resulting in an experience cluttered by ads and forced software updates.

Apple is using lower-priced hardware as a "Trojan horse" to acquire first-time users, prioritizing long-term revenue from Apple Services like iCloud and AppleTV+ over immediate hardware profit margins.
summary:
Marques Brownlee discusses the disruptive impact of Apple’s latest laptop releases, specifically the high-performance M5 Max MacBook Pro and the budget-friendly $600 MacBook Neo. While the MacBook Pro sets new benchmarks for professional-grade power—surpassing previous desktop-class performance—the Neo is highlighted as a "shock to the industry." By offering Apple’s signature premium build quality, vertical integration, and efficiency at a price point previously dominated by budget Windows PCs, Apple is directly challenging the value proposition of the entire Windows ecosystem.

The transcript highlights a fundamental struggle on the Windows side, which Brownlee compares to the fragmentation of Android. While Windows laptops like the Dell XPS 14 offer impressive hardware and variety, their success relies on the perfect alignment of hardware manufacturers, chipmakers like Intel, and Microsoft’s software. Brownlee argues that Microsoft is currently the "weak link," cluttering the user experience with forced updates, third-party ads, and controversial AI features like Recall. This lack of cohesion makes it difficult for Windows machines to feel as "premium" or optimized as MacBooks, even at higher price points.

Ultimately, Brownlee views the MacBook Neo as a strategic "Trojan Horse" designed to capture first-time Mac users and pull them into Apple’s lucrative services ecosystem. Because Apple can prioritize long-term service revenue over immediate hardware margins, they can offer a level of quality that budget Windows manufacturers—who operate on thin margins and fragmented supply chains—cannot easily match. This shift signals a potential market share crisis for Windows brands if they cannot find a way to streamline their software experience and compete with Apple's aggressive new pricing strategy.
